title: Go With The Flow
description: Execute an assembly program where the instruction pointer is bound to a register; analytically shortcut the Part 2 sum-of-divisors loop.
writeup:
## Approach

The device has 6 registers and 16 opcodes (same as Day 16). One register is designated as the instruction pointer (`#ip N`): before each instruction the IP is written into that register, and after execution the register's value is written back — enabling jumps via arithmetic.

## Part 1

Simulate the program from `r[0]=0` until the IP goes out of bounds. Return `r[0]`.

## Part 2

Starting with `r[0]=1` causes the initialization code to produce a much larger target number N (~10 million for most inputs). The main loop then iterates over all pairs `(i, j)` checking `i*j == N` — an O(N²) brute-force sum-of-divisors that would take hours to simulate.

**Shortcut:** run the program only until any register first exceeds 1000. At that point initialization is complete and N is sitting in the largest register. Then compute the sum of divisors directly in O(√N):

```python
i = 1
while i * i <= N:
    if N % i == 0:
        total += i + (N // i if i != N // i else 0)
    i += 1
```

## Visualization

Shows the disassembly, then traces the first 50 instruction steps of Part 1 (register values before/after each instruction), and finally reveals N and its divisors for Part 2.
